APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper studies a central topic in representation learning for NLP — how context types and representations affect word embeddings — and generalizes widely-used models (Skip-Gram, CBOW, GloVe) to arbitrary context types (implemented in word2vecPM). It uses accepted datasets and evaluation protocols (Wikipedia training data, WordSim353, Google analogies, CoNLL POS/Chunking, CoNLL NER, downstream classification) and reports appropriate metrics, matching community standards. The audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in embeddings and downstream robustness) and problem formulation are directly aligned with top-tier venue scope. Overall the work is well positioned for a top NLP/ML conference. | B(5): The paper studies word embedding design choices (linear vs dependency contexts; bound vs unbound representations) and generalizes Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary context types, which is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope. It targets the right audience by evaluating on standard intrinsic and extrinsic benchmarks (WordSim353, Google analogies, CoNLL POS/chunking/NER, multiple sentiment/text-classification datasets) and comparing to relevant baselines (GloVe, Levy & Goldberg DEPS). The problem formulation, methodology (training on Wikipedia, parsing with CoreNLP), and evaluation metrics are those expected at these venues, and the paper is positioned appropriately with adequate related work and stated limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper studies a central topic in representation learning for NLP — how context types and representations affect word embeddings — and generalizes widely-used models (Skip-Gram, CBOW, GloVe) to arbitrary context types (implemented in word2vecPM). It uses accepted datasets and evaluation protocols (Wikipedia training data, WordSim353, Google analogies, CoNLL POS/Chunking, CoNLL NER, downstream classification) and reports appropriate metrics, matching community standards. The audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in embeddings and downstream robustness) and problem formulation are directly aligned with top-tier venue scope. Overall the work is well positioned for a top NLP/ML conference.

It targets the right audience by evaluating on standard intrinsic and extrinsic benchmarks (WordSim353, Google analogies, CoNLL POS/chunking/NER, multiple sentiment/text-classification datasets) and comparing to relevant baselines (GloVe, Levy & Goldberg DEPS). The problem formulation, methodology (training on Wikipedia, parsing with CoreNLP), and evaluation metrics are those expected at these venues, and the paper is positioned appropriately with adequate related work and stated limitations.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodology (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early (generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary contexts and comparing context types/representations). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and figures/tables and evaluation metrics are described sufficiently to support the claims. Writing quality is described as good with adequate related-work coverage, so only minor presentation issues remain (e.g., some conditional claims about dependency/context representation interactions could be emphasized more clearly). | B(4): The summary indicates the paper is well-organized (methods, experiments, conclusions laid out) and states the main contributions early and clearly (effects of context types/representations, generalization of SG/CBOW/GloVe). Notation and experimental setup appear consistent and well-specified (datasets, metrics, hyperparameters reported), and figures/tables/examples seem effective given the thorough intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations. Writing quality is described as good and limitations are explicitly acknowledged, though a few presentation details (e.g., justification/impact of the 100-occurrence vocabulary cutoff and explicit mention of figure/table content) could be more prominently highlighted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodology (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early (generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary contexts and comparing context types/representations). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and figures/tables and evaluation metrics are described sufficiently to support the claims. Writing quality is described as good with adequate related-work coverage, so only minor presentation issues remain (e.g., some conditional claims about dependency/context representation interactions could be emphasized more clearly).

reviewer_b.justification: The summary indicates the paper is well-organized (methods, experiments, conclusions laid out) and states the main contributions early and clearly (effects of context types/representations, generalization of SG/CBOW/GloVe). Notation and experimental setup appear consistent and well-specified (datasets, metrics, hyperparameters reported), and figures/tables/examples seem effective given the thorough intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—how context type (linear vs dependency-based) and context representation (bound vs unbound) affect embeddings—and provides actionable findings (e.g., dependency-based + bound better for functional similarity and for sequence-labeling tasks like POS/chunking/NER; linear/unbound better for topical relatedness). By generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary contexts (word2vecPM) and showing that intrinsic evaluations do not reliably predict downstream performance (and that GloVe is more sensitive to representation choices), the results are likely to change how researchers and practitioners select or design embedding setups. This should stimulate follow-up work exploring context-representation interactions and yield widely used practical guidance, though it is not a transformative theoretical advance. | B(4): The paper addresses an important, practical question—how context type (linear vs dependency-based) and context representation (bound vs unbound) affect learned embeddings—and provides systematic empirical evidence across intrinsic benchmarks and multiple downstream tasks (POS, chunking, NER, classification). Its findings (e.g., dependency contexts with bound representations better capture functional similarity and improve sequence-labeling tasks; linear contexts better capture topical relatedness; GloVe’s sensitivity to context representation) give actionable guidance likely to change how researchers choose and evaluate embedding setups. By generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary contexts (word2vecPM) and highlighting intrinsic vs extrinsic evaluation mismatches, it also opens follow-up work on context-aware embedding design and evaluation. The contribution is broadly useful to the NLP embedding community, though not strictly transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—how context type (linear vs dependency-based) and context representation (bound vs unbound) affect embeddings—and provides actionable findings (e.g., dependency-based + bound better for functional similarity and for sequence-labeling tasks like POS/chunking/NER; linear/unbound better for topical relatedness). By generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary contexts (word2vecPM) and showing that intrinsic evaluations do not reliably predict downstream performance (and that GloVe is more sensitive to representation choices), the results are likely to change how researchers and practitioners select or design embedding setups. This should stimulate follow-up work exploring context-representation interactions and yield widely used practical guidance, though it is not a transformative theoretical advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important, practical question—how context type (linear vs dependency-based) and context representation (bound vs unbound) affect learned embeddings—and provides systematic empirical evidence across intrinsic benchmarks and multiple downstream tasks (POS, chunking, NER, classification). Its findings (e.g., dependency contexts with bound representations better capture functional similarity and improve sequence-labeling tasks; linear contexts better capture topical relatedness; GloVe’s sensitivity to context representation) give actionable guidance likely to change how researchers choose and evaluate embedding setups. By generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary contexts (word2vecPM) and highlighting intrinsic vs extrinsic evaluation mismatches, it also opens follow-up work on context-aware embedding design and evaluation. The contribution is broadly useful to the NLP embedding community, though not strictly transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs a broad set of controlled experiments: it generalizes Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary context collections, compares linear vs dependency contexts and bound vs unbound representations, and evaluates across both intrinsic (WordSim353, analogies) and extrinsic tasks (POS, chunking, NER, several text-classification sets). It also reports hyperparameters and discusses sensitivity (e.g., GloVe’s greater sensitivity and the dependency-bound interaction) and several limitations (vocabulary cutoff, intrinsic–extrinsic mismatches). Where it falls short is in not reporting code availability and lacking some deeper ablations (e.g., effects of parsing quality, corpus size/lower frequency thresholds), so the experimental coverage is strong but not exhaustive. | B(4): The paper runs a broad set of experiments across intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogies) and extrinsic tasks (POS, chunking, NER, multiple text-classification corpora) and compares multiple models (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW and GloVe) with different context types and representations, which provides strong empirical support for the main claims. The authors include sensitivity analyses (e.g., dependence of dependency-based contexts on bound representations and GloVe’s higher sensitivity) and vary key factors (context type/representation and model family), addressing ablation-style questions. Related work is described as adequate and several limitations/negative results are discussed (mismatch between intrinsic and downstream tasks; the effect of filtering contexts/words appearing <100 times). Reproducibility is partially supported by reported hyperparameters and dataset details, but lack of mentioned code and the aggressive frequency cutoff limit full reproducibility and coverage, so some minor gaps remain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs a broad set of controlled experiments: it generalizes Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to arbitrary context collections, compares linear vs dependency contexts and bound vs unbound representations, and evaluates across both intrinsic (WordSim353, analogies) and extrinsic tasks (POS, chunking, NER, several text-classification sets). It also reports hyperparameters and discusses sensitivity (e.g., GloVe’s greater sensitivity and the dependency-bound interaction) and several limitations (vocabulary cutoff, intrinsic–extrinsic mismatches). Where it falls short is in not reporting code availability and lacking some deeper ablations (e.g., effects of parsing quality, corpus size/lower frequency thresholds), so the experimental coverage is strong but not exhaustive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs a broad set of experiments across intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogies) and extrinsic tasks (POS, chunking, NER, multiple text-classification corpora) and compares multiple models (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW and GloVe) with different context types and representations, which provides strong empirical support for the main claims. The authors include sensitivity analyses (e.g., dependence of dependency-based contexts on bound representations and GloVe’s higher sensitivity) and vary key factors (context type/representation and model family), addressing ablation-style questions. Related work is described as adequate and several limitations/negative results are discussed (mismatch between intrinsic and downstream tasks; the effect of filtering contexts/words appearing <100 times). Reproducibility is partially supported by reported hyperparameters and dataset details, but lack of mentioned code and the aggressive frequency cutoff limit full reproducibility and coverage, so some minor gaps remain.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW, GloVe, and the DEPS dependency-based method) and evaluates all variants on the same Wikipedia training data and shared intrinsic/extrinsic metrics with hyperparameters reported. It explicitly acknowledges when prior choices perform comparably or better (e.g., dependency contexts help mainly with bound representations; GloVe is more sensitive) and analyzes differences between context types and representations across multiple downstream tasks. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the experimental setup appears fair and comprehensive given the provided summary. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW, GloVe, and prior DEPS dependency-based work) and evaluates across many standard intrinsic and extrinsic benchmarks (WordSim353, Google analogies, CoNLL POS/chunking, NER, multiple text‑classification sets) trained on the same Wikipedia corpus. Comparisons appear fair: the same data and evaluation metrics are used, hyperparameters are reported, and the authors analyze when baselines perform comparably or worse (e.g., GloVe’s sensitivity to context representation and the conditional benefit of dependency contexts only with bound representations). The paper also cites and discusses relevant competing approaches (Levy & Goldberg DEPS) and provides clear analysis of differences between their generalized models and prior methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW, GloVe, and the DEPS dependency-based method) and evaluates all variants on the same Wikipedia training data and shared intrinsic/extrinsic metrics with hyperparameters reported. It explicitly acknowledges when prior choices perform comparably or better (e.g., dependency contexts help mainly with bound representations; GloVe is more sensitive) and analyzes differences between context types and representations across multiple downstream tasks. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the experimental setup appears fair and comprehensive given the provided summary.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW, GloVe, and prior DEPS dependency-based work) and evaluates across many standard intrinsic and extrinsic benchmarks (WordSim353, Google analogies, CoNLL POS/chunking, NER, multiple text‑classification sets) trained on the same Wikipedia corpus. Comparisons appear fair: the same data and evaluation metrics are used, hyperparameters are reported, and the authors analyze when baselines perform comparably or worse (e.g., GloVe’s sensitivity to context representation and the conditional benefit of dependency contexts only with bound representations). The paper also cites and discusses relevant competing approaches (Levy & Goldberg DEPS) and provides clear analysis of differences between their generalized models and prior methods.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by a comprehensive experimental suite (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe implementations, comparisons to DEPS, evaluations on WordSim353, Google analogies and multiple downstream tasks using Wikipedia-trained embeddings). There are no complex mathematical proofs required beyond the described generalizations, and nothing in the summary suggests incorrect derivations. The experimental methodology is broadly sound — appropriate baselines, intrinsic and extrinsic tasks, and reported hyperparameters — but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and notes a strong vocabulary/context-frequency cutoff (discarding items <100 occurrences) that may limit coverage and generality. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only minor methodological caveats that do not overturn the main findings. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by a comprehensive experimental evaluation across intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogies) and extrinsic (POS, chunking, NER, text classification) tasks using generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW and GloVe variants and appropriate baselines (including dependency-based DEPS and linear contexts). Mathematical aspects are limited to model generalization and are consistent with established algorithms; no derivation errors are indicated in the summary. Methodologically it is largely sound, but there are minor concerns (e.g., aggressive filtering of words/contexts appearing <100 times that could bias vocabulary/coverage, and no mention of statistical significance testing or code release). Overall the results and qualified limitations reported support the stated conclusions, so only minor issues remain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by a comprehensive experimental suite (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe implementations, comparisons to DEPS, evaluations on WordSim353, Google analogies and multiple downstream tasks using Wikipedia-trained embeddings). There are no complex mathematical proofs required beyond the described generalizations, and nothing in the summary suggests incorrect derivations. The experimental methodology is broadly sound — appropriate baselines, intrinsic and extrinsic tasks, and reported hyperparameters — but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and notes a strong vocabulary/context-frequency cutoff (discarding items <100 occurrences) that may limit coverage and generality. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only minor methodological caveats that do not overturn the main findings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by a comprehensive experimental evaluation across intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogies) and extrinsic (POS, chunking, NER, text classification) tasks using generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW and GloVe variants and appropriate baselines (including dependency-based DEPS and linear contexts). Mathematical aspects are limited to model generalization and are consistent with established algorithms; no derivation errors are indicated in the summary. Methodologically it is largely sound, but there are minor concerns (e.g., aggressive filtering of words/contexts appearing <100 times that could bias vocabulary/coverage, and no mention of statistical significance testing or code release). Overall the results and qualified limitations reported support the stated conclusions, so only minor issues remain.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a systematic empirical study by generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to accept arbitrary context types (implemented as word2vecPM) and comparing linear vs dependency contexts and bound vs unbound representations across intrinsic and extrinsic tasks. While this yields useful novel findings (e.g., dependency contexts help functional similarity only with bound representations; bound reps aid sequence-labeling), the core methods build on well-known techniques (e.g., DEPS/Levy & Goldberg) and are largely comparative/engineering rather than a fundamentally new modeling idea. Overall the contribution is incremental with moderate novelty in the observed context × representation interactions. | B(3): The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new modeling paradigm (it builds on Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe and prior dependency-context work such as DEPS) but provides a non-trivial, systematic generalization (word2vecPM) to accept arbitrary context types and a thorough empirical comparison. Its main novelty lies in evaluating context type (linear vs dependency) and context representation (bound vs unbound) across intrinsic and extrinsic tasks and highlighting practical findings (e.g., dependency+bound benefits functional similarity and downstream tasks; GloVe sensitivity). These are useful empirical contributions and clearer differentiation from prior work, but overall represent an incremental advance rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a systematic empirical study by generalizing Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe to accept arbitrary context types (implemented as word2vecPM) and comparing linear vs dependency contexts and bound vs unbound representations across intrinsic and extrinsic tasks. While this yields useful novel findings (e.g., dependency contexts help functional similarity only with bound representations; bound reps aid sequence-labeling), the core methods build on well-known techniques (e.g., DEPS/Levy & Goldberg) and are largely comparative/engineering rather than a fundamentally new modeling idea. Overall the contribution is incremental with moderate novelty in the observed context × representation interactions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new modeling paradigm (it builds on Skip-Gram/CBOW/GloVe and prior dependency-context work such as DEPS) but provides a non-trivial, systematic generalization (word2vecPM) to accept arbitrary context types and a thorough empirical comparison. Its main novelty lies in evaluating context type (linear vs dependency) and context representation (bound vs unbound) across intrinsic and extrinsic tasks and highlighting practical findings (e.g., dependency+bound benefits functional similarity and downstream tasks; GloVe sensitivity). These are useful empirical contributions and clearer differentiation from prior work, but overall represent an incremental advance rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

main_claims: Different context types (linear vs dependency-based) and context representations (bound vs unbound) substantially affect learned word embeddings and their evaluation outcomes.

Dependency-based (syntactic) contexts better capture functional similarity (e.g., WordSim353 similarity), but this benefit depends on using bound context representations; linear (bag-of-words) contexts better capture topical/relatedness similarity, especially with unbound representations.

Bound context representations and dependency-based contexts tend to improve performance on sequence labeling downstream tasks (POS tagging, chunking, NER); bound representations generally outperform unbound for these extrinsic tasks.

The paper generalizes Skip-Gram (GSG), CBOW (GBOW) and GloVe to accept arbitrary context types (implemented as word2vecPM), and shows that GloVe is more sensitive to context representation choices than Skip-Gram or CBOW.
_summary.methodology: Extend/generalize Skip-Gram, CBOW and GloVe to accept arbitrary context collections (linear bag-of-words and dependency-based syntactic contexts), implement these in word2vecPM, train embeddings on English Wikipedia (parsed with Stanford CoreNLP), and evaluate across intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogies) and extrinsic (POS, chunking, NER, text classification) tasks varying context type and representation.
datasets: Google analogy dataset (semantic and syntactic partitions)
limitations: Dependency-based contexts capture functional similarity primarily when used with bound context representations (dependency type acknowledged as a required condition).

Intrinsic evaluations (word similarity and analogy) do not necessarily correlate with downstream/extrinsic task performance, so results on intrinsic tasks may not translate to typical downstream uses.
GloVe is reported to be more sensitive to context representation choices than Skip-Gram and CBOW, indicating stability issues across configurations.
Training ignores words and contexts that appear fewer than 100 times in the extracted collection P, which affects the vocabulary and may limit coverage.
